unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit, you will explore the exciting world of space and discovery. You will read about how people study the stars, build amazing projects, and work together to achieve great things. Along the way, you will learn new vocabulary, practise important grammar skills, and develop your writing and speaking abilities.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Discuss evidence and language choices. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise two to three paragraphs. Present, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners investigating space, discovery and teamwork

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The ability to take care of yourself and make your own choices

The word independence belongs to our vocabulary group 1 vocabulary.
I can explain independence in my own words.
I can connect independence to this unit's texts.
I can use independence in a clear Grade 5 sentence.

readings.title: Exploring Space: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit, you will explore the exciting world of space and discovery. You will read about how people study the stars, build amazing projects, and work together to achieve great things. Along the way, you will learn new vocabulary, practise important grammar skills, and develop your writing and speaking abilities.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Use and understand 25 new vocabulary words related to systems, science, and social values
Identify and use indefinite pronouns such as everyone, somebody, nothing, and anything
Read and understand an informative passage about space exploration
Write a blog post using informal language and personal reflections
Distinguish between facts and opinions in a text
Use adverbs and adverbial phrases to add detail to your sentences
Practise speaking and listening skills through role-play and discussion
Understand the difference between biographies and autobiographies
Before you begin reading, take a moment to look at the important words you will meet in this unit. Knowing these words will help you understand the texts and complete the activities. Read through the table below, say each word aloud, and try to use it in a short sentence of your own.
Reading: The Night Sky Above Us
Read the passage below carefully. Think about the main ideas and any new words as you read.
For thousands of years, people have looked up at the night sky and wondered what lies beyond the clouds. In ancient times, astronomers in the Muslim world made brilliant discoveries. Scholars in Baghdad, Cairo, and Timbuktu built observatories, mapped the stars, and invented instruments to observe the heavens more clearly. Their work became the source of much of the knowledge we use in modern astronomy today.
One of the most important early astronomers was Al-Sufi, who lived in Persia over a thousand years ago. He wrote a famous book called the Book of Fixed Stars, which described the positions and brightness of hundreds of stars. His observations were so accurate that everyone who studied the sky after him used his work as a guide. Nobody in Europe had created anything like it at the time.

readings.title: Exploring Space: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Centuries later, the dream of exploring space became real. In 1957, the first artificial satellite, Sputnik 1, was launched into orbit around the Earth. This was a booming moment in history, and it changed everything. Scientists began to cooperate across borders, sharing ideas and working together to reach the Moon. In 1969, astronauts landed on the Moon for the first time. Someone once said that this was the greatest achievement of the twentieth century.
Today, the International Space Station (ISS) orbits our planet. Astronauts from many countries live and work there. They conduct experiments, grow food, and study how the human body reacts to living in space. The towering rockets that carry them into orbit are marvels of engineering. Everything on the station must be carefully planned because there is no shop in space! If anything breaks, the crew must fix it themselves.
Space exploration is not just about science. It teaches us the value of cooperation, patience, and hard work. It shows us that when people avoid conflict and work towards a shared goal, they can achieve brilliant results. Whether you dream of becoming an astronomer, an engineer, or a teacher, the stars above remind us that there is always something new to discover.
As the famous saying goes, the sky is not the limit; it is only the beginning.
Now that you have read the passage, answer the following questions. Write your answers in full sentences.
The author writes that the stars remind us there is always something new to discover. Do you agree? Give a reason for your answer.
Find one fact and one opinion in the passage. Write them down and explain how you can tell the difference.
Go back to the reading passage and find the words listed below. For each word, explain what it means based on the way it is used in the passage. Write your answer in a full sentence.
When you read a text, it is important to be able to tell the difference between facts and opinions. This is a key skill that will help you become a better reader and thinker.

readings.title: The Burrow Discovery, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Read the story below carefully. As you read, look out for the vocabulary words you have been learning and notice how the characters speak and work together. Enjoy the adventure!
In a quiet highland village in Ethiopia, a small school sat between green hills and rocky paths. Inside the school, excitement buzzed. This was the week of the Science Systems Fair, and everyone in Year 5 had to present a working science display.
At the centre of the classroom, Noah and her team stood beside their table. "Our project is about animal homes," she explained. "We'll build a model of a burrow, show how animals live inside it, and explain why it's important."
Samira, her classmate, adjusted the small object on top of their model. "This is our soil sensor," he said proudly. "It tells us how moist the ground is."
"You mean when it works," teased Selma. "It didn't work at all yesterday."
Samira rolled his eyes. "We fixed it. You'll see."
Later that day, the teacher made an announcement. "Students, listen up. There's a booming science blog competition. You'll write about your project, and winners will present at the town library!"
Noah smiled. "Only if everyone does their part."
They started typing their blog post using the school tablets. Noah wrote:
"Our group of learners studied animal homes. We built a burrow model. It was hard work, but we cooperated well."
"You'll want to avoid using cheap glue! Our tunnel collapsed twice!"
"We discovered a glowing object in the field while collecting materials. At first, we misunderstood what it was. We thought it was plastic, but it turned out to be part of a meteor!"
Noah laughed. "Don't forget to clearly explain that part. It sounds like a science movie."

readings.title: The Burrow Discovery, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
They revised and used contractions: you'll, we've, they're. The blog sounded fun and real.
Later, the class voted on the best blog post. Another group presented about wind energy. "Our towering windmill model produces light," they said. "The glare from the bulbs shows it works!"
"The glare hurts my eyes," someone whispered.
Samira sighed. "Our project doesn't light up. Maybe we'll lose."
"You should relax," Noah said. "We're not in an empire. This is school, not war."
Selma giggled. "Still, a win would be nice."
When the fair day arrived, families from different regions came. The head teacher gave a speech. "Today, we celebrate cooperation, observation, and hard work."
"No one will," said Selma. "Unless we destroy something."
Their model worked. The burrow stayed standing, and the sensor beeped at the right time.
After the presentations, a visitor approached their table. "I'm from the National Science Youth Council," she said. "Where did this source idea come from?"
"It started when we saw rabbits dig near the road," Noah said. "We wanted to expose how animals create cool, dark places to rest -- especially in this hot season."
"I was also inspired by a film about energy," Selma added. "And the sensor just came to me."
"You seem very loyal to your work," the visitor said. "That's important in science."
Later, the team gathered to hear the blog results. The teacher smiled. "The winning team is... Noah, Selma, and Selma!"
"You'll present next week at the library," she said. "But you'll also join a local energy project. Your task is to reduce glare from public lights and consume less electricity."
"Another project?" Selma asked, feeling drowsy.
"You mustn't nap yet," Selma said. "This is a great chance!"

readings.title: The Burrow Discovery, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"Okay, okay," Selma said. "But I want snacks first."
The next day, they were walking to the community hall when they saw students outside, holding signs.
"They're starting a boycott against plastic cups in the lunchroom," someone said. "They want to protect the environment."
"I don't know," said Selma. "I'd rather research it first."
"We should support it," Noah said. "But let's find out more."
So they talked to the group leader. He showed them the data -- hundreds of cups used weekly, none recycled.
"You've got our approval," said Noah. "We'll help announce it next week."
That afternoon, they worked on their new blog:
"Today, we joined a group of students to start a boycott of plastic cups. We cooperated, shared ideas, and wrote a plan. Not everyone agrees yet, but we're working on it."
"We also learned: not everything in science goes fast. You must be patient. But when you mix loyalty, effort, and curiosity -- the results can be brilliant."
Now that you have read The Burrow Discovery, take some time to reflect on the story. Answer the questions below in full sentences.

grammar.title: What Are Indefinite Pronouns?
grammar.explanation:
Indefinite pronouns are special words that we use when we want to talk about people or things in a general way, without being specific. They are called 'indefinite' because they do not point to a definite or particular person or thing.
For example, instead of saying:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Indefinite pronouns are special words that we use when we want to talk about people or things in a general way, without being specific. They are called 'indefinite' because they do not point to a definite or particular person or thing.
For example, instead of saying:
"Noah and Selma and Selma all cooperated during the experiment."
We can say:
"Everyone cooperated during the experiment."
The word everyone replaces the specific names and refers to all the people in the group. This makes our writing shorter, smoother, and more flexible.
Indefinite pronouns are very important because they help us:
Talk about people or things when we do not know exactly who or what they are
Make general statements about groups
Ask questions about unknown people or things
Make negative statements clearly and naturally
Types of Indefinite Pronouns
Pronouns for People
These indefinite pronouns are used when we are talking about people:
Pronouns for Things
These indefinite pronouns are used when we are talking about things:
Pronouns for Quantity
These indefinite pronouns tell us about how many or how much:
Important Rules to Remember
Rule 1: Singular Verb Agreement
Most indefinite pronouns that refer to people or things (everyone, somebody, nothing, etc.) are treated as singular. This means they need a singular verb (is, has, was, does, knows, etc.).
Correct examples:
Everyone is ready for the presentation.
Nobody knows where the book went.
Something was glowing in the field.
Everything has been checked twice.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are indefinite pronouns? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common mistakes:
grammar.explanation:
Everyone are happy. --> Everyone is happy.
Nobody know the answer. --> Nobody knows the answer.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Everyone are happy. --> Everyone is happy.
Nobody know the answer. --> Nobody knows the answer.
Something were strange. --> Something was strange.
Rule 2: Someone vs. Anyone
Use someone / somebody / something in positive sentences.
Use anyone / anybody / anything in questions and negative sentences.
Examples:
Someone called you this morning. (positive)
Did anyone call me? (question)
I did not hear anything. (negative)
Something is wrong with the computer. (positive)
Is anything wrong? (question)
Rule 3: No One and Nothing Are Already Negative
The pronouns no one, nobody, and nothing already carry a negative meaning. Do not add another negative word (like 'not') to the same sentence, or you will create a double negative.
Correct:
Nobody was late for class.
There is nothing in the box.
Incorrect (double negative):
Nobody was not late for class.
There is not nothing in the box.
Rule 4: Using 'Their' with Singular Indefinite Pronouns
In modern English, it is common and accepted to use their to refer back to a singular indefinite pronoun like everyone, someone, or nobody. This avoids awkward phrases like 'his or her'.
Everyone should bring their own lunch.
Somebody left their backpack in the classroom.
Nobody wanted to miss their chance to present.
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes: in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Practice Exercises
grammar.explanation:
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun to complete each sentence.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Choose the correct indefinite pronoun to complete each sentence.
__________ wants to go outside for break. (Everyone / Anyone)
Does __________ know where the library book is? (someone / anyone)
__________ was missing from the science kit. (Something / Anything)
__________ in the class passed the test -- it was too difficult. (No one / Anyone)
We looked everywhere, but we could not find __________. (something / anything)
__________ of the students finished early. (Few / Nothing)
__________ is more important than working together. (Nothing / Anything)
Has __________ seen my notebook? (somebody / anybody)
__________ knew the answer except the teacher. (Nobody / Anybody)
__________ must bring their permission slip by Friday. (Everyone / Anyone)
Exercise 2: Correct the Sentences
Each sentence has a mistake. Rewrite it correctly.
Everyone are invited to the school fair.
Nobody don't know the answer.
Does someone have a spare pen?
Everything have been packed for the trip.
I can't find nothing in my bag.
Somebody have eaten my lunch.
Exercise 3: Someone, Anyone, or No One?
Fill in each blank with someone, anyone, or no one.
__________ should check the equipment before we start.
Is __________ coming to the meeting today?
__________ remembered to bring the map.
I don't think __________ noticed the mistake.
__________ volunteered to clean the classroom.
__________ in the group has ever visited a space centre.
Exercise 4: Write Your Own
Write six sentences of your own, each using a different indefinite pronoun from the list below. Make sure your sentences are about school, science, or your community.
Pronouns to use: everyone, something, nobody, anything, many, few
Exercise 5: Story Hunt
Go back to the story The Burrow Discovery and find five examples of indefinite pronouns. For each one:
Write the sentence from the story.
Underline the indefinite pronoun.
Explain whether the pronoun refers to a person, a thing, or a quantity.

grammar.practice: Apply practice exercises in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Grade 5 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read a paragraph aloud. Check sentence completeness, agreement, tense, joining words and punctuation. Revise one feature at a time.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.
grammar.practice: Edit a two-paragraph response using the unit language focus. Mark four changes and explain how two changes improve clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Astronaut Interview Role-Play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Work with a partner or ask your AI Tutor to role-play an interview. One person plays a journalist and the other plays an astronaut who has just returned from a space mission.
The journalist should ask at least five questions. Here are some ideas:
What was the most brilliant discovery you made in space?
Was there anything that surprised you about living on the ISS?
How did the crew cooperate during difficult moments?
Did everyone on the crew have the same job?
What advice would you give to someone who wants to become an astronaut?
The astronaut should answer in full sentences, using vocabulary from this unit and at least three indefinite pronouns.
Activity 2: Fact or Opinion?
Your teacher or AI Tutor will read out a list of statements about space. After each statement, decide whether it is a fact (something that can be proven true) or an opinion (what someone thinks or believes). Hold up a card or say your answer aloud.
Here are some statements to get you started:
The Moon orbits the Earth.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise astronaut interview role-play and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Group Presentation -- Our Space Mission
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
In small groups, plan a pretend space mission. Give your mission a name and decide:
Where will you go? (the Moon, Mars, or beyond?)
What will you study or discover?
What equipment will you need?
How will the team cooperate to make the mission a success?
Each group member should present one part of the mission plan to the class. Use at least three vocabulary words and two indefinite pronouns in your presentation.
Activity 4: Adverb Challenge
Adverbs and adverbial phrases add detail to our sentences by telling us when, where, how, or how much something happens. In this activity, you will practise adding adverbs to make your sentences more interesting.
Look at the plain sentences below. Rewrite each one by adding an adverb or adverbial phrase from the box. Then read your improved sentence aloud to a partner.
Adverb box: in 1969, carefully, on the Moon, with great effort, finally, every day, at the space centre, patiently
The astronauts landed. --> The astronauts landed __________.
Scientists study the stars. --> Scientists study the stars __________.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise group presentation -- our space mission and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1:
writing.promptAndInstructions: In this section, you will use what you have learned about vocabulary, grammar, and reading to produce your own piece of writing. Take your time and enjoy the creative process!
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about .
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan topic sentences and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence structures, compare ideas, improve transitions and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: A Space Discovery Blog Post
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine that you are a young astronaut writing a blog post about your first week on the International Space Station. Your blog should be written in an informal, friendly tone -- as if you are chatting with your friends and family back home.
Your blog post should include:
At least five vocabulary words from this unit (underline them)
At least four indefinite pronouns (circle them or write them in bold)
Contractions to make your writing sound natural (e.g. I'm, we've, it's, you'll)
A description of something surprising you discovered in space
A short paragraph about how the crew cooperates to get things done
A closing sentence that encourages your readers to keep exploring and learning
Your blog post should be between 150 and 250 words long.
Planning Your Blog Post
Before you start writing, it helps to plan your ideas. Use these questions to guide your thinking:
What is the most exciting thing that happened during your first week?

activities.title: Activities 1: Choose the Correct Pronoun
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Fill in each blank with the best indefinite pronoun from the box below.
Word box: everyone, somebody, nothing, anything, everything, nobody, something, few
__________ in our class enjoys reading about space.
Is there __________ you would like to ask the teacher?
__________ left a glowing object near the field.
There is __________ wrong with the experiment. It worked perfectly!
__________ must be packed before we leave for the trip.
__________ has ever been to the Moon from our village.
I think __________ mysterious is happening in the lab.
Only a __________ of the students had seen a real rocket.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Correct the Mistakes
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Each sentence below has a mistake with the indefinite pronoun or the verb. Rewrite the sentence correctly.
Everyone are excited about the science fair.
Nobody know the answer to the question.
Everything have been cleaned in the lab.
Someone have left their lunch in the cafeteria.
Do anyone want to help with the project?
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Write Your Own Sentences
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write a sentence using each of the following indefinite pronouns. Make sure your sentence is about space, science, or discovery!
everyone
something
nobody
anything
many
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Someone or Anyone?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the correct pronoun to complete each sentence. Remember: use someone/somebody in positive sentences and anyone/anybody in questions and negatives.
__________ should check the sensor before the presentation. (someone / anyone)
Does __________ have a spare pencil I can borrow? (someone / anyone)
I do not think __________ noticed the mistake. (someone / anyone)
__________ has been using the school tablets today. (Someone / Anyone)
Can __________ explain how the satellite works? (someone / anyone)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5:
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Everyone in our class enjoys reading about space.
Is there anything you would like to ask the teacher?
Somebody left a glowing object near the field.
There is nothing wrong with the experiment. It worked perfectly!
Everything must be packed before we leave for the trip.
Nobody has ever been to the Moon from our village.
I think something mysterious is happening in the lab.
Only a few of the students had seen a real rocket.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.instructionsAndItems:

quizzes.question: What does 'independence' mean?
quizzes.options: A large area of land that is controlled by one ruler or government | A very bright light that is hard to see in | The ability to take care of yourself and make your own choices | To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known
quizzes.correctAnswer: The ability to take care of yourself and make your own choices
quizzes.explanation: On her birthday, she felt proud of her independence because she could choose her own cake.

quizzes.question: What does 'issue' mean?
quizzes.options: A place or thing where something comes from | A problem or topic that people talk about or need to think about | A very bright light that is hard to see in | To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known
quizzes.correctAnswer: A problem or topic that people talk about or need to think about
quizzes.explanation: We had a big issue with our school project, so we worked together to fix it.

quizzes.question: What does 'empire' mean?
quizzes.options: A large area of land that is controlled by one ruler or government | A place or thing where something comes from | A very bright light that is hard to see in | To work together with others to achieve something
quizzes.correctAnswer: A large area of land that is controlled by one ruler or government
quizzes.explanation: The Roman Empire was very powerful and included many countries.

quizzes.question: What does 'expose' mean?
quizzes.options: A place or thing where something comes from | To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known | To work together with others to achieve something | When people decide not to buy or use something to show they disagree
quizzes.correctAnswer: To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known
quizzes.explanation: The teacher will expose the secret to the class during the lesson.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Burrow Discovery, part 2': "We wanted to _____ how animals create cool, dark places to rest -- especially in this hot season." "I was also inspired by a film about energy," Selma added.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: "We wanted to expose how animals create cool, dark places to rest -- especially in this hot season." "I was also inspired by a film about energy," Selma added.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Burrow Discovery, part 2': Your task is to reduce _____ from public lights and consume less electricity." "Another project?" Selma asked, feeling drowsy.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Your task is to reduce glare from public lights and consume less electricity." "Another project?" Selma asked, feeling drowsy.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Burrow Discovery, part 2': "Where did this _____ idea come from?" "It started when we saw rabbits dig near the road," Noah said.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: "Where did this source idea come from?" "It started when we saw rabbits dig near the road," Noah said.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Exploring Space: source text 2': Scientists began to _____ across borders, sharing ideas and working together to reach the Moon.

quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Burrow Discovery, part 3': "They're starting a _____ against plastic cups in the lunchroom," someone said.

quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Burrow Discovery, part 2': "And the sensor just came to me." "You seem very _____ to your work," the visitor said.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: "And the sensor just came to me." "You seem very loyal to your work," the visitor said.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are unsure about any word or idea in this lesson, ask your AI Tutor.
Try saying: What does the word 'cooperate' mean? or Can you give me an example of an indefinite pronoun?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you found any question difficult, try asking your AI Tutor for a hint.
Say: Can you help me find a fact and an opinion in the passage about space?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
If you are confused about when to use 'someone' versus 'anyone', try asking:
When do I use 'someone' and when do I use 'anyone' in a sentence?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Need help starting your blog? Try asking:
Can you help me write an opening sentence for a blog about living in space?
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
Want to check your adverb sentences? Try saying:
Can you check whether I have used adverbs of time, place, and manner correctly?

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

The ability to take care of yourself and make your own choices On her birthday, she felt proud of her independence because she could choose her own cake.
A problem or topic that people talk about or need to think about We had a big issue with our school project, so we worked together to fix it.
A large area of land that is controlled by one ruler or government The Roman Empire was very powerful and included many countries.
To show something that is usually hidden or to make something known The teacher will expose the secret to the class during the lesson.
A very bright light that is hard to see in The glare from the sun made it hard to see the road.
A place or thing where something comes from The river is the source of the water for our town.
To work together with others to achieve something We need to cooperate to finish the project on time.
When people decide not to buy or use something to show they disagree The students decided to boycott the cafeteria food because they wanted healthier options.
Always being there for someone or something you care about My dog is very loyal; he always stays by my side.
To completely ruin or make something unusable The storm will destroy the old tree in our yard.
